text: Migraine is commonly associated with pulsatile headache, photophobia, nausea, and sensitivity to sound.
Tension headaches generally produce bilateral pressure without neurological symptoms.
Photophobia combined with unilateral pulsatile pain is strongly correlated with migraine episodes.
Cluster headaches present severe orbital pain and autonomic symptoms such as tearing.
Hypertension may cause headaches accompanied by dizziness and blurred vision.
Meningitis symptoms include neck stiffness, fever, and intense headache.
Sinusitis frequently causes facial pressure and pain around the nasal cavity.
Patients with migraine often report worsening symptoms in bright environments.
Temporal arteritis may lead to scalp tenderness and visual impairment.
Neurological deficits combined with headache may indicate stroke.
Photophobia is a neurological symptom frequently observed in migraine diagnostics.
Severe dehydration may trigger diffuse headache and cognitive fatigue.
Brain tumors can produce persistent headaches resistant to analgesics.
Patients experiencing migraine may prefer dark and quiet environments.
Vertigo associated with headaches can indicate vestibular migraine.
Occipital neuralgia causes sharp pain radiating from the neck to the scalp.
Migraine aura may include temporary visual disturbances before pain onset.
Chronic stress contributes significantly to recurrent tension headaches.
Sleep deprivation is a common trigger for migraine crises.
Nausea and sensitivity to light are classic migraine-associated symptoms.
